content.body: Rectangles and triangles were easy. But what is the area under a genuine curve like $f(x) = x^2$? The trick is to slice the region into many thin strips, treat each as a skinny rectangle, and add them up. The thinner the strips, the better the estimate.
Make the strips infinitely thin and the running total becomes exact. That exact area is the definite integral.
Here is $f(x) = x^2$. Drag $x$ to the right and watch the shaded area pile up. As $x$ grows, the area grows…

options: not at all — it stays the same

feedback.correct: Right — because the curve gets taller as $x$ grows, each new strip is taller than the last, so the area piles up faster and faster.
feedback.incorrect: Watch how much area each step adds. As the curve rises, the new strips get taller.
feedback.hint: Taller strips add more area. Since $x^2$ keeps rising, the area grows faster and faster.

content.body: Adding up infinitely many strips by hand would be painful, so instead we run the power rule in reverse. An antiderivative of $f$ is a function $F$ whose derivative gives $f$ back: $F'(x) = f(x)$.
To reverse the power rule on a power of $x$, undo each step: add 1 to the exponent, then divide by that new exponent. This is the reverse power rule.

For $f(x) = x^2$, bump the exponent up to 3 and divide by 3, giving the antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$. Check it forward: differentiating $\frac{x^3}{3}$ gives $\frac{3x^2}{3} = x^2$, right back to $f$.
To get the area under $f$ between two points $x = a$ and $x = b$, evaluate the antiderivative at each endpoint and subtract:
Use the antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$. What is the area under $f(x) = x^2$ from 0 to 2? Evaluate $F(2) - F(0)$. (Round to two decimals.)

feedback.incorrect: Evaluate the antiderivative at the endpoints: $F(2) - F(0) = \frac{2^3}{3} - 0$.

content.body: Back to the graph of $f(x) = x^2$. Find the value of $t$ for which the area from 0 to $t$ equals 9, then drag the slider there. (Remember: the antiderivative is $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$, so the area from 0 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3}{3}$.)

feedback.correct: Nice — the area from 0 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3}{3}$, and $\frac{3^3}{3} = 9$, so $t = 3$. You located exactly where 9 units of area have accumulated.
feedback.incorrect: The area from 0 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3}{3}$. Set $\frac{t^3}{3} = 9$, so $t^3 = 27$. Solve for $t$, then drag the slider there.

content.body: For $f(x) = x^2$, suppose you double the interval from $[0, b]$ to $[0, 2b]$. The area…

feedback.correct: Right — the area is $\frac{b^3}{3}$, so doubling $b$ gives $\frac{(2b)^3}{3} = 8 \cdot \frac{b^3}{3}$. The area grows eightfold, far more than double.
feedback.incorrect: The area depends on $b^3$, not $b$. What happens to $b^3$ when you double $b$?
feedback.hint: $(2b)^3 = 8b^3$, so the area becomes 8 times as big — more than double.

content.body: You now know that area under a curve is built from thin strips and captured exactly by the definite integral — and that reversing the power rule produces an antiderivative like $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$ that hands you the area.
But why should undoing a derivative give an area at all? That surprising link between slopes and areas is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — coming up next.
Using the antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$, what is the area under $f(x) = x^2$ from 0 to 3?
What is the area under $f(x) = x^2$ from 0 to 1? (Round to two decimals.)
To approximate the area under a curve, we add up many thin…

feedback.correct: Correct — we slice the region into thin rectangles (strips) and sum their areas.
feedback.incorrect: Think about the shape of each thin slice under the curve.
feedback.hint: Each thin strip is treated as a skinny rectangle.
